content.text: is a fundamental concept in information theory that quantifies how much information one random variable reveals about another—it measures the

about one variable when you know the value of another, capturing any kind of dependency (linear or non-linear) between variables and serving as a powerful tool for feature selection, clustering, and understanding relationships in machine learning.
What it is — Clear definition and intuition

= entropy of X (uncertainty in X before knowing Y)
-

= conditional entropy of X given Y (uncertainty in X after knowing Y)
-
= reduction in uncertainty about X when Y is known
I(X; Y) = Σ Σ P(x, y) × log(P(x, y) / (P(x) × P(y)))

= joint distribution (actual relationship between X and Y)
-

= product of marginals (what joint would look like if X and Y were independent)
-
= how much the actual relationship differs from independence
I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y)
= joint entropy of X and Y together

Mutual Information = how much one variable actually reveals about another

Example: Weather puzzle
- X = "Is it raining?" (uncertain)
- Y = "Are people using umbrellas?" (uncertain)

I(X; Y) = how much knowing umbrella usage reduces uncertainty about rain [web:326]

Unlike correlation, it doesn't care whether the relationship is linear — it picks up on any kind of dependency, continuous or discrete [web:326]
: "Mutual information measures how much information is communicated, on average, in one random variable about another".

H(X)          H(Y)
    ┌─────┐       ┌─────┐
    │     │       │     │
    │     │  I(X;Y)     │
    │  X  │◄──────► Y  │
    │     │       │     │
    └─────┘       └─────┘
    
I(X;Y) = shared information between X and Y [web:326]

H(X|Y) = uncertainty in X after knowing Y (only X part)
H(Y|X) = uncertainty in Y after knowing X (only Y part)

H(X, Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X) = H(Y) + H(X|Y)

From this:
H(X) - H(X|Y) = H(Y) - H(Y|X) [web:325]

This difference = mutual information = reduction in uncertainty when another variable is known [web:325]
I(X; Y) = 0 if and only if X and Y are statistically independent
I(X; Y) = H(X) when Y completely determines X (perfect correlation)
: "Mutual information between two variables is 0 if and only if the two variables are statistically independent".

Mutual Information = KL Divergence between joint distribution and product of marginals

I(X; Y) = KL(p(x, y) || p(x)p(y)) [web:325][web:329][web:331]

I(X; Y) = Σ Σ P(x, y) × log(P(x, y) / (P(x) × P(y)))

       = KL(p(x,y) || p(x)p(y))

This is the KL divergence between the joint distribution and what the joint would look like if the variables were independent [web:325]

Intuitively, if X, Y are independent, p(x, y) = p(x)p(y), and the KL-divergence is zero, and knowing X gives zero information gain about Y [web:325]

: "It's the KL divergence between the joint distribution and what the joint would look like if the variables were independent. How far is the actual relationship from pure independence? That's what mutual information measures".

Joint distribution P(X, Y):

            Y=umbrella  Y=no umbrella
X=rain         0.4         0.1
X=no rain      0.2         0.3

Marginals:
P(X=rain) = 0.4 + 0.1 = 0.5
P(X=no rain) = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5
P(Y=umbrella) = 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6
P(Y=no umbrella) = 0.1 + 0.3 = 0.4

Step 1: Compute joint entropy H(X, Y)
H(X, Y) = -Σ Σ P(x,y) × log(P(x,y))
        = -[0.4×log(0.4) + 0.1×log(0.1) + 0.2×log(0.2) + 0.3×log(0.3)]
        = 1.56 bits

Step 2: Compute marginal entropies
H(X) = -[0.5×log(0.5) + 0.5×log(0.5)] = 1.0 bits
H(Y) = -[0.6×log(0.6) + 0.4×log(0.4)] = 0.97 bits

Step 3: Compute mutual information
I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y)
        = 1.0 + 0.97 - 1.56
        = 0.41 bits

Interpretation: Knowing umbrella usage reduces uncertainty about rain by 0.41 bits

X = coin flip, Y = another coin flip (independent)

P(X=H) = 0.5, P(X=T) = 0.5
P(Y=H) = 0.5, P(Y=T) = 0.5
P(X,Y) = P(X) × P(Y) (independent)

H(X) = 1.0 bits
H(Y) = 1.0 bits
H(X, Y) = 2.0 bits

I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y)
        = 1.0 + 1.0 - 2.0
        = 0 bits

Interpretation: Variables are independent → zero mutual information ✓ [web:324][web:330]

X = Y (identical variables)

P(X=H, Y=H) = 0.5
P(X=T, Y=T) = 0.5

H(X) = 1.0 bits
H(Y) = 1.0 bits
H(X, Y) = 1.0 bits (same information)

I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y)
        = 1.0 + 1.0 - 1.0
        = 1.0 bits

Interpretation: Knowing X completely determines Y → maximum mutual information ✓

import numpy as np

# Joint probability matrix P(X, Y)
# Rows: X values, Columns: Y values
P_xy = np.array([[0.4, 0.1],  # X=rain
                 [0.2, 0.3]])  # X=no rain

# Marginals
P_x = P_xy.sum(axis=1)  # [0.5, 0.5]
P_y = P_xy.sum(axis=0)  # [0.6, 0.4]

# Mutual Information
mi = 0
for i in range(P_xy.shape[0]):
    for j in range(P_xy.shape [people.cs.umass](https://people.cs.umass.edu/~elm/Teaching/Docs/mutInf.pdf)):
        if P_xy[i, j] > 0:
            mi += P_xy[i, j] * np.log(P_xy[i, j] / (P_x[i] * P_y[j]))

print(f"Mutual Information: {mi:.3f} nats")  # ≈ 0.594 nats

from sklearn.metrics import mutual_info_regression, mutual_info_classif

# For continuous variables
mi = mutual_info_regression(X, y)

# For discrete variables
mi = mutual_info_classif(X, y)
Why it matters — Importance in Generative AI
Select features with highest MI to target variable
Evaluate clustering quality using MI between clusters
Train models to maximize MI between hidden layer and labels
Measure dependency between latent variables and outputs
Select features that preserve most MI with target
: Feature selection in ML uses MI to identify which features provide the most information about the target class, independent of whether relationships are linear or non-linear.
: "Unlike correlation, it doesn't care whether the relationship is linear — it picks up on any kind of dependency, continuous or discrete".
: I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X|Y) = reduction in uncertainty about X when Y known
: I(X; Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X, Y)
: How much one variable reveals about another
: I(X; Y) = 0 iff X and Y are statistically independent
: How far actual relationship is from pure independence
: Works for any dependency (linear or non-linear), discrete or continuous
: Select features with highest MI to target
: Maximize MI between hidden layer and labels
: I(X; Y) = H(X) when Y completely determines X
: Information communicated on average in one variable about another

For Generative AI, Mutual Information is fundamental: it's used for feature selection to identify informative features, evaluating clustering quality in latent spaces, measuring dependency between latent variables and generated outputs in VAEs/GANs, and implementing the information bottleneck principle to train models that maximize information about labels while minimizing redundancy—making it essential for understanding how variables relate and how models learn meaningful representations; understanding Mutual Information is critical for mastering how neural networks capture dependencies and learn informative representations.
